1:
"And the LORD said unto Moses, Speak unto the priests the sons of Aaron, and say unto them, There shall none be defiled for the dead among his people:" The witnesses note the opening of the law of priestly holiness — the sons of Aaron not to defile themselves for the dead. The witnesses note that Gill sets it that, as care was taken for the holiness of all Israel, much more the priests, who served God especially, must keep from all defilement. The witnesses note that the Jewish writers connect this section with the foregoing law on familiar spirits, teaching men to seek the priest in matters of doubt, not the diviner. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the to the sons of Aaron — the law addressed to the priests; the none defiled for the dead — not to contract the defilement of corpses; the a stricter separation — the priest held to a higher purity than the people; the for God's service — kept clean for the ministry of the living God.

The witnesses agree the priest's nearness to God required a stricter separation than the people's: he who ministered in holy things was not to contract the defilement of the dead at every funeral, but to keep himself clean for the service of the living God — a holiness that foreshadows the great High Priest, holy and undefiled (Heb. 7:26).

So the witnesses make the verse the law of priestly purity: The witnesses note the to the sons of Aaron: the law addressed to the priests. The witnesses note the none defiled for the dead: not to contract the defilement of corpses. The witnesses note the a stricter separation: the priest held to a higher purity than the people. The witnesses note the for God's service: kept clean for the ministry of the living God. The expositors observe the to the sons of Aaron: the law addressed to the priests. The expositors observe the none defiled for the dead: not to contract the defilement of corpses. The expositors observe the a stricter separation: the priest held to a higher purity than the people. The expositors observe the for God's service: kept clean for the ministry of the living God. The believer is taught to mark the to the sons of Aaron, the none defiled for the dead, the a stricter separation, the for God's service, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priest's nearness to God required a stricter holiness — kept from the defilement of the dead for the service of the living God, foreshadowing Christ undefiled.
2:
"But for his kin, that is near unto him, that is, for his mother, and for his father, and for his son, and for his daughter, and for his brother," The witnesses note the exception of mercy: for his nearest kin the priest might defile himself, mourning and attending their burial. The witnesses note that Gill lists the near kin for whom he might be defiled, and takes the opening clause to include also his wife, his own flesh, the nearest of all. The witnesses note that the law of holiness did not forbid the priest natural affection, but bounded it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his nearest — mother, father, son, daughter, brother; the and his wife — his own flesh, the nearest of all, supposed though not named; the mourning allowed — the priest might be defiled for these and attend their burial; the affection ordered — natural grief bounded by holiness, not extinguished; the humanity in the law — the priest not made a stranger to the grief God himself gave.

The witnesses agree the law tempered its strictness with humanity: the priest was not made a stranger to natural grief, but might mourn and bury father, mother, child, and brother — the holiness required of him not extinguishing the affections God himself had given, but only ordering them.

So the witnesses make the verse the near kin excepted: The witnesses note the his nearest: mother, father, son, daughter, brother. The witnesses note the and his wife: his own flesh, the nearest of all, supposed though not named. The witnesses note the mourning allowed: the priest might be defiled for these and attend their burial. The witnesses note the affection ordered: natural grief bounded by holiness, not extinguished. The witnesses note the humanity in the law: the priest not made a stranger to the grief God himself gave. The expositors observe the his nearest: mother, father, son, daughter, brother. The expositors observe the and his wife: his own flesh, the nearest of all, supposed though not named. The expositors observe the mourning allowed: the priest might be defiled for these and attend their burial. The expositors observe the affection ordered: natural grief bounded by holiness, not extinguished. The expositors observe the humanity in the law: the priest not made a stranger to the grief God himself gave. The believer is taught to mark the his nearest, the and his wife, the mourning allowed, the affection ordered, the humanity in the law, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priest might mourn his nearest kin — the law's holiness ordering natural affection, not destroying it.
3:
"And for his sister a virgin, that is nigh unto him, which hath had no husband; for her may he be defiled." The witnesses note the virgin sister added to the near kin — she who, unmarried, still belonged to her father's house. The witnesses note that Gill takes the nearness as nearness of place as well as kin, the unmarried sister remaining in her father's house till death. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads her as the maiden sister still in sole relationship with him, who when married would pass to her husband's house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a virgin sister — one who has had no husband; the nigh unto him — near in place as in kin, still in the father's house; the mourning allowed — for her the priest might be defiled; the the unmarried — reckoned his own, having no husband to mourn her.

The witnesses agree the unmarried sister was reckoned among the priest's own because she had no husband to mourn her: the law followed the natural ties of the household, granting the priest leave to grieve for her whom marriage had not yet removed to another family — mercy reaching to the sister left in his care.

So the witnesses make the verse the virgin sister: The witnesses note the a virgin sister: one who has had no husband. The witnesses note the nigh unto him: near in place as in kin, still in the father's house. The witnesses note the mourning allowed: for her the priest might be defiled. The witnesses note the the unmarried: reckoned his own, having no husband to mourn her. The expositors observe the a virgin sister: one who has had no husband. The expositors observe the nigh unto him: near in place as in kin, still in the father's house. The expositors observe the mourning allowed: for her the priest might be defiled. The expositors observe the the unmarried: reckoned his own, having no husband to mourn her. The believer is taught to mark the a virgin sister, the nigh unto him, the mourning allowed, the the unmarried, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priest might mourn the virgin sister still in his house — the law following the natural ties of the household in its mercy.
4:
"But he shall not defile himself, being a chief man among his people, to profane himself." The witnesses note the limit set: beyond the nearest kin the priest, a chief man among his people, was not to defile himself. The witnesses note that Gill rejects the readings that make this a noble or a husband, and interprets it of a priest, who was not to defile himself for kin more remote, such as those related only by marriage. The witnesses note that the priest's dignity as one set among the people forbade his profaning himself by needless mourning. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a chief man — the priest set in dignity among his people; the not defile himself — not for kin more remote than the nearest; the lest he profane himself — his office not to be made common by needless grief; the holiness' boundary — a limit fixed where affection might wander.

The witnesses agree the verse draws the boundary of the priest's grief: his office, making him a chief and consecrated man, would be profaned were he to defile himself for the more distant dead; holiness fixed a limit where natural affection might have wandered, that the man of God not be common.

So the witnesses make the verse the limit of mourning: The witnesses note the a chief man: the priest set in dignity among his people. The witnesses note the not defile himself: not for kin more remote than the nearest. The witnesses note the lest he profane himself: his office not to be made common by needless grief. The witnesses note the holiness' boundary: a limit fixed where affection might wander. The expositors observe the a chief man: the priest set in dignity among his people. The expositors observe the not defile himself: not for kin more remote than the nearest. The expositors observe the lest he profane himself: his office not to be made common by needless grief. The expositors observe the holiness' boundary: a limit fixed where affection might wander. The believer is taught to mark the a chief man, the not defile himself, the lest he profane himself, the holiness' boundary, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priest was not to defile himself beyond his nearest kin — his consecrated office fixing a limit on grief, that the man of God not be made common.
5:
"They shall not make baldness upon their head, neither shall they shave off the corner of their beard, nor make any cuttings in their flesh." The witnesses note the forbidding of the heathen mourning customs — baldness, shaving, and cuttings — to the priests as to the people. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were superstitious customs of the heathen in mourning for the dead, the Chaldeans, Greeks, and Egyptians shaving and disfiguring themselves. The witnesses note that what was forbidden to all Israel (Lev. 19:27-28) was the more forbidden the priests. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no baldness — the head not shaved for the dead; the no shaved beard — the corner of the beard not marred; the no cuttings — the flesh not gashed in grief; the the heathen custom — the despair of the nations forbidden the priest.

The witnesses agree the priest above all was to be free of the frantic, self-wounding grief of those who had no hope: his very body was to bear no mark of heathen despair, for he stood before the God of the living and was to mourn, if at all, as one who trusted the LORD, not as the nations who knew him not.

So the witnesses make the verse against heathen mourning: The witnesses note the no baldness: the head not shaved for the dead. The witnesses note the no shaved beard: the corner of the beard not marred. The witnesses note the no cuttings: the flesh not gashed in grief. The witnesses note the the heathen custom: the despair of the nations forbidden the priest. The expositors observe the no baldness: the head not shaved for the dead. The expositors observe the no shaved beard: the corner of the beard not marred. The expositors observe the no cuttings: the flesh not gashed in grief. The expositors observe the the heathen custom: the despair of the nations forbidden the priest. The believer is taught to mark the no baldness, the no shaved beard, the no cuttings, the the heathen custom, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priest was to bear no mark of heathen despair — his grief, if any, that of one who trusts the LORD, not the self-wounding of the nations.
6:
"They shall be holy unto their God, and not profane the name of their God: for the offerings of the LORD made by fire, and the bread of their God, they do offer: therefore they shall be holy." The witnesses note the reason of all these laws — the priests are holy unto their God, offering his bread, and must not profane his name. The witnesses note that Gill presses that the priests, given wholly to God's service, should be clean and pure who bear the vessels of the Lord, and an example of holiness to others. The witnesses note that Ellicott gives this as the very reason they may not disfigure themselves: being sacred to the Lord, they are not to indulge heathen grief. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the holy unto their God — the priests wholly given to his service; the not profane his name — their conduct not to dishonour him; the they offer his bread — handling the offerings of the LORD; the therefore holy — the holiness of the service requiring holy servants.

The witnesses agree the ground of priestly holiness is the holiness of their service: those who handle the offerings of God and present the bread of his table must themselves be holy, lest they profane his name in the very act of serving him — a charge the New Testament lays on all who bear the vessels of the Lord (2 Tim. 2:21).

So the witnesses make the verse the ground of holiness: The witnesses note the holy unto their God: the priests wholly given to his service. The witnesses note the not profane his name: their conduct not to dishonour him. The witnesses note the they offer his bread: handling the offerings of the LORD. The witnesses note the therefore holy: the holiness of the service requiring holy servants. The expositors observe the holy unto their God: the priests wholly given to his service. The expositors observe the not profane his name: their conduct not to dishonour him. The expositors observe the they offer his bread: handling the offerings of the LORD. The expositors observe the therefore holy: the holiness of the service requiring holy servants. The believer is taught to mark the holy unto their God, the not profane his name, the they offer his bread, the therefore holy, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priests must be holy because they handle the holy things of God — lest they profane his name in serving him, a charge on all who bear the vessels of the Lord.
7:
"They shall not take a wife that is a whore, or profane; neither shall they take a woman put away from her husband: for he is holy unto his God." The witnesses note the law of priestly marriage — not to take a harlot, a profaned woman, or a divorced wife. The witnesses note that Gill distinguishes the "whore" who prostitutes herself from the "profane" whose chastity was violated, and notes the various Jewish glosses on each. The witnesses note that the divorced woman also was forbidden the priest, that his marriage match his holiness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not a whore — no harlot to be the priest's wife; the nor a profane woman — none whose chastity was violated; the nor a divorced wife — the put-away woman forbidden him; the for he is holy — his marriage to match his consecration.

The witnesses agree the priest's very household was to be holy: the one who ministered before God was not to take a wife whose name or chastity was stained, for his holiness reached into his marriage and his seed; the partner of his life was to be without reproach, as he was set apart unto his God.

So the witnesses make the verse priestly marriage: The witnesses note the not a whore: no harlot to be the priest's wife. The witnesses note the nor a profane woman: none whose chastity was violated. The witnesses note the nor a divorced wife: the put-away woman forbidden him. The witnesses note the for he is holy: his marriage to match his consecration. The expositors observe the not a whore: no harlot to be the priest's wife. The expositors observe the nor a profane woman: none whose chastity was violated. The expositors observe the nor a divorced wife: the put-away woman forbidden him. The expositors observe the for he is holy: his marriage to match his consecration. The believer is taught to mark the not a whore, the nor a profane woman, the nor a divorced wife, the for he is holy, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priest's marriage was bound by his holiness — no stained or divorced wife, his very household to be without reproach as he was set apart unto God.
8:
"Thou shalt sanctify him therefore; for he offereth the bread of thy God: he shall be holy unto thee: for I the LORD, which sanctify you, am holy." The witnesses note the charge to the people to sanctify the priest — to esteem him holy and uphold his holiness, because he offers the bread of God. The witnesses note that Gill reads "sanctify him" of thinking and speaking well of him, esteeming him holy, and even of the magistrate using his authority to keep the priesthood from unlawful marriages. The witnesses note that the ground is given again — God who sanctifies them is himself holy. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sanctify him — the people to esteem and uphold the priest as holy; the he offers the bread of God — the reason of the honour due him; the holy unto thee — his office guarded from scandal; the the LORD who sanctifies — god himself the holy fountain of all holiness.

The witnesses agree the people had a part in the priest's holiness: they were to honour his office, guard it from scandal, and not draw him into what would defile it; and the closing word lifts all to its source — the LORD who sanctifies his people is himself holy, the fountain of every holiness required (Lev. 20:8).

So the witnesses make the verse the people's part: The witnesses note the sanctify him: the people to esteem and uphold the priest as holy. The witnesses note the he offers the bread of God: the reason of the honour due him. The witnesses note the holy unto thee: his office guarded from scandal. The witnesses note the the LORD who sanctifies: God himself the holy fountain of all holiness. The expositors observe the sanctify him: the people to esteem and uphold the priest as holy. The expositors observe the he offers the bread of God: the reason of the honour due him. The expositors observe the holy unto thee: his office guarded from scandal. The expositors observe the the LORD who sanctifies: God himself the holy fountain of all holiness. The believer is taught to mark the sanctify him, the he offers the bread of God, the holy unto thee, the the LORD who sanctifies, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the people were to uphold the priest's holiness, honouring his office — all grounded in the LORD who sanctifies and is himself holy.
9:
"And the daughter of any priest, if she profane herself by playing the whore, she profaneth her father: she shall be burnt with fire." The witnesses note the grave sentence on a priest's daughter who played the harlot — she profaned her father, and was to be burned. The witnesses note that Gill notes the law applies whether she were betrothed or married, and that the burning was a more terrible death than the stoning of others guilty of the like. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the daughter's whoredom as a profaning of her father, whose holy office her sin disgraced. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the plays the whore — the daughter profaning herself; the profanes her father — her sin disgracing his holy office; the burnt with fire — a heavier death than others' stoning; the the closer watch — those nearest the altar held to the strictest holiness.

The witnesses agree the heavier penalty measured the heavier dishonour: the sin that would be grievous in any was the more so in the house of a priest, for it dragged his sacred office into shame; holiness required that those nearest the altar bear the closest watch, their fall the deepest scandal.

So the witnesses make the verse the priest's daughter: The witnesses note the plays the whore: the daughter profaning herself. The witnesses note the profanes her father: her sin disgracing his holy office. The witnesses note the burnt with fire: a heavier death than others' stoning. The witnesses note the the closer watch: those nearest the altar held to the strictest holiness. The expositors observe the plays the whore: the daughter profaning herself. The expositors observe the profanes her father: her sin disgracing his holy office. The expositors observe the burnt with fire: a heavier death than others' stoning. The expositors observe the the closer watch: those nearest the altar held to the strictest holiness. The believer is taught to mark the plays the whore, the profanes her father, the burnt with fire, the the closer watch, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the priest's daughter who played the harlot bore a heavier penalty — her sin profaning his sacred office, holiness watching most closely over those nearest the altar.
10:
"And he that is the high priest among his brethren, upon whose head the anointing oil was poured... shall not uncover his head, nor rend his clothes;" The witnesses note the yet stricter law of the high priest — the anointed head not to be uncovered, the garments not rent, even in grief. The witnesses note that Gill notes the high priest is greater than his brethren, set above them in office, the anointing oil poured on his head. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch mark him consecrated by the anointing and the holy garments, and so bound to a holiness above the rest. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the greater than his brethren — set above the priests in office; the the anointing oil poured — consecrated upon his head; the uncover not his head — not to bear the common token of grief; the rend not his clothes — the crown of consecration upon him.

The witnesses agree the high priest, as the highest type of the coming Mediator, was held to the strictest separation of all: he was not even to bear the common tokens of mourning, the uncovered head and rent robe, for the crown of his consecration was upon him; his unbroken composure foreshadowed the great High Priest whose holiness no sorrow could stain (Heb. 7:26).

So the witnesses make the verse the high priest's holiness: The witnesses note the greater than his brethren: set above the priests in office. The witnesses note the the anointing oil poured: consecrated upon his head. The witnesses note the uncover not his head: not to bear the common token of grief. The witnesses note the rend not his clothes: the crown of consecration upon him. The expositors observe the greater than his brethren: set above the priests in office. The expositors observe the the anointing oil poured: consecrated upon his head. The expositors observe the uncover not his head: not to bear the common token of grief. The expositors observe the rend not his clothes: the crown of consecration upon him. The believer is taught to mark the greater than his brethren, the the anointing oil poured, the uncover not his head, the rend not his clothes, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the high priest was held to the strictest separation, not even to mourn as others — the crown of consecration upon him, foreshadowing the High Priest whose holiness no sorrow could stain.
11:
"Neither shall he go in to any dead body, nor defile himself for his father, or for his mother;" The witnesses note the high priest forbidden to approach any dead body at all — not even for his own father or mother. The witnesses note that Gill explains he was not to enter a house where a dead body lay, lest he be unclean seven days and so hindered from his office, and notes the heathen later aped this in their priest of Jupiter. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the high priest's separation above even the deepest natural ties. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the any dead body — not to enter where the dead lay; the not his father — not even for his own father; the nor his mother — nor for his mother might he be defiled; the consecration over nature — his holiness above the closest natural ties.

The witnesses agree the high priest's consecration overruled the closest claims of nature: where the common priest might mourn his parents, the high priest might not, for his unbroken holiness was the very type of his office; the service of the living God so wholly possessed him that even a parent's death could not draw him from the sanctuary into defilement.

So the witnesses make the verse no defilement at all: The witnesses note the any dead body: not to enter where the dead lay. The witnesses note the not his father: not even for his own father. The witnesses note the nor his mother: nor for his mother might he be defiled. The witnesses note the consecration over nature: his holiness above the closest natural ties. The expositors observe the any dead body: not to enter where the dead lay. The expositors observe the not his father: not even for his own father. The expositors observe the nor his mother: nor for his mother might he be defiled. The expositors observe the consecration over nature: his holiness above the closest natural ties. The believer is taught to mark the any dead body, the not his father, the nor his mother, the consecration over nature, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the high priest might not defile himself even for father or mother — his consecration overruling the closest claims of nature, wholly possessed by the service of God.
12:
"Neither shall he go out of the sanctuary, nor profane the sanctuary of his God; for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him: I am the LORD." The witnesses note the high priest not to leave the sanctuary in his service nor profane it, the crown of anointing oil upon him. The witnesses note that Gill explains he was not to go out of the sanctuary in the time of service to follow the dead, the law respecting him in the case of his dead; his honour was to remain in the sanctuary. The witnesses note that the crown of the anointing oil marks him as set wholly apart to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the go not out — not to leave his service for the dead; the profane not the sanctuary — his absence would dishonour God's house; the the crown of anointing oil — the consecration that crowns him; the wholly God's — belonging so to the sanctuary that no grief may call him forth.

The witnesses agree the high priest belonged so wholly to God's house that no private grief might call him from it: to leave his service for the dead would profane the sanctuary, for the oil of consecration crowned him; his unbroken attendance pictures the abiding ministry of Christ, who ever lives in the true sanctuary to intercede (Heb. 7:25).

So the witnesses make the verse bound to the sanctuary: The witnesses note the go not out: not to leave his service for the dead. The witnesses note the profane not the sanctuary: his absence would dishonour God's house. The witnesses note the the crown of anointing oil: the consecration that crowns him. The witnesses note the wholly God's: belonging so to the sanctuary that no grief may call him forth. The expositors observe the go not out: not to leave his service for the dead. The expositors observe the profane not the sanctuary: his absence would dishonour God's house. The expositors observe the the crown of anointing oil: the consecration that crowns him. The expositors observe the wholly God's: belonging so to the sanctuary that no grief may call him forth. The believer is taught to mark the go not out, the profane not the sanctuary, the the crown of anointing oil, the wholly God's, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the high priest was bound to the sanctuary, not to be called from it by grief — his unbroken ministry a picture of Christ who ever lives to intercede (Heb. 7:25).
13:
"And he shall take a wife in her virginity." The witnesses note the high priest's marriage held to the strictest rule — one wife, and she a virgin. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jewish teachers held the high priest might take but one wife, "a wife," one and not two, though polygamy was practised by others and even by common priests. The witnesses note that the wife was to be a virgin, never known nor even betrothed to another. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a wife — one only, not two, even where others took many; the in her virginity — one never known nor betrothed to another; the the purest house — the high priest's marriage without stain; the the type — answering the charge of one wife (1 Tim. 3:2).

The witnesses agree the high priest's house was to be the purest of all: a single, undefiled marriage, that the highest type of the coming Mediator be without stain even in his domestic life; Gill marks how the rule answers to the New Testament charge that the minister be the husband of one wife (1 Tim. 3:2).

So the witnesses make the verse the high priest's wife: The witnesses note the a wife: one only, not two, even where others took many. The witnesses note the in her virginity: one never known nor betrothed to another. The witnesses note the the purest house: the high priest's marriage without stain. The witnesses note the the type: answering the charge of one wife (1 Tim. 3:2). The expositors observe the a wife: one only, not two, even where others took many. The expositors observe the in her virginity: one never known nor betrothed to another. The expositors observe the the purest house: the high priest's marriage without stain. The expositors observe the the type: answering the charge of one wife (1 Tim. 3:2). The believer is taught to mark the a wife, the in her virginity, the the purest house, the the type, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the high priest was to take one virgin wife — his house the purest of all, the highest type of the Mediator unstained even in domestic life.
14:
"A widow, or a divorced woman, or profane, or an harlot, these shall he not take: but he shall take a virgin of his own people to wife." The witnesses note the women forbidden to the high priest — widow, divorced, profaned, harlot — he must take a virgin of his own people. The witnesses note that Gill notes the high priest might not marry even a widow, whether of a priest or an Israelite, nor one merely betrothed and so left a widow. The witnesses note that the rule was stricter than for the common priest, who might marry a widow. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not a widow — barred to the high priest, though not the common priest; the nor divorced or profane — none whose state was stained; the nor a harlot — the unchaste utterly forbidden; the a virgin of his people — the high priest to wed only the undefiled.

The witnesses agree the high priest's separation reached into his marriage more strictly than any: where the common priest was barred only the harlot, profane, and divorced, the high priest was barred the widow also, that nothing in his household carry the least shadow — his domestic purity the deepest of all, befitting his place before God.

So the witnesses make the verse forbidden marriages: The witnesses note the not a widow: barred to the high priest, though not the common priest. The witnesses note the nor divorced or profane: none whose state was stained. The witnesses note the nor a harlot: the unchaste utterly forbidden. The witnesses note the a virgin of his people: the high priest to wed only the undefiled. The expositors observe the not a widow: barred to the high priest, though not the common priest. The expositors observe the nor divorced or profane: none whose state was stained. The expositors observe the nor a harlot: the unchaste utterly forbidden. The expositors observe the a virgin of his people: the high priest to wed only the undefiled. The believer is taught to mark the not a widow, the nor divorced or profane, the nor a harlot, the a virgin of his people, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the high priest might wed only a virgin of his own people — his domestic purity stricter than any priest's, befitting his place before God.
15:
"Neither shall he profane his seed among his people: for I the LORD do sanctify him." The witnesses note the reason of the strict law — lest by an unworthy marriage the high priest profane his seed, degrading his offspring. The witnesses note that Gill notes that by such marriages his children would lie under disgrace, unfit to succeed him in the priesthood. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads it that he is not to contract any forbidden marriage, lest the children of such an issue, like their mother, be debarred the priesthood and its portions. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the profane not his seed — no marriage to degrade his offspring; the unfit to succeed — the children of such a match barred the priesthood; the the line guarded — the holiness transmitted unbroken to his seed; the the LORD sanctifies him — the holiness God's own gift and claim.

The witnesses agree the holiness of the priesthood was to be transmitted unbroken: an unfit marriage would taint not the high priest only but the line that came after, barring them from the altar; and the closing word grounds all in the LORD who sanctifies him — the holiness required being God's own gift and claim, not man's invention.

So the witnesses make the verse the unprofaned seed: The witnesses note the profane not his seed: no marriage to degrade his offspring. The witnesses note the unfit to succeed: the children of such a match barred the priesthood. The witnesses note the the line guarded: the holiness transmitted unbroken to his seed. The witnesses note the the LORD sanctifies him: the holiness God's own gift and claim. The expositors observe the profane not his seed: no marriage to degrade his offspring. The expositors observe the unfit to succeed: the children of such a match barred the priesthood. The expositors observe the the line guarded: the holiness transmitted unbroken to his seed. The expositors observe the the LORD sanctifies him: the holiness God's own gift and claim. The believer is taught to mark the profane not his seed, the unfit to succeed, the the line guarded, the the LORD sanctifies him, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the high priest's marriage guarded the holiness of his line — an unfit match profaning his seed, all grounded in the LORD who sanctifies him.
16:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the opening of a new section — the law of bodily blemishes that barred a priest from offering. The witnesses note that Gill notes that after the holiness of the priests as to defilement and marriage, God adds laws concerning blemishes in their bodies that rendered them unfit for service. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch see the principle that, as the spiritual nature is reflected in the bodily form, only a faultless body could answer to the holiness of the priest. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the bodily blemishes — imperfections that unfit a priest for service; the body and spirit — the outward form answering the inward holiness; the the nations' instinct — even the heathen required whole priests.

The grammatical commentators (Keil and Delitzsch) note that even the Greeks and Romans, for the same instinct, required their priests to be whole and unmaimed; the law of Israel raised this to a sacred principle — the outward soundness of the priest a parable of the inward perfection that befits those who draw near to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of blemishes: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the bodily blemishes: imperfections that unfit a priest for service. The witnesses note the body and spirit: the outward form answering the inward holiness. The witnesses note the the nations' instinct: even the heathen required whole priests. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the bodily blemishes: imperfections that unfit a priest for service. The expositors observe the body and spirit: the outward form answering the inward holiness. The expositors observe the the nations' instinct: even the heathen required whole priests. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the bodily blemishes, the body and spirit, the the nations' instinct, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that God added the law of bodily blemishes — the priest's outward soundness a parable of the inward perfection that befits those who draw near to God.
17:
"Speak unto Aaron, saying, Whosoever he be of thy seed in their generations that hath any blemish, let him not approach to offer the bread of his God." The witnesses note the rule: no descendant of Aaron with a blemish might approach to offer the bread of God. The witnesses note that Gill notes Aaron, as high priest, was to see the rules observed and to examine who might serve. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the general law over vv.17-23, the blemished priest not to offer the bread of his God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the whosoever of thy seed — any descendant of Aaron; the that hath any blemish — marked by any bodily defect; the let him not approach — not to draw near to the altar; the to offer the bread of God — the dignity of the service guarded.

The witnesses agree the law guarded the dignity of the altar: he who presented the offerings of God before all the people was to be without visible defect, that nothing unseemly mar the holy service; the requirement points beyond itself to the one perfect Priest, without blemish of body or soul, who offered himself to God (Heb. 9:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the blemished barred: The witnesses note the whosoever of thy seed: any descendant of Aaron. The witnesses note the that hath any blemish: marked by any bodily defect. The witnesses note the let him not approach: not to draw near to the altar. The witnesses note the to offer the bread of God: the dignity of the service guarded. The expositors observe the whosoever of thy seed: any descendant of Aaron. The expositors observe the that hath any blemish: marked by any bodily defect. The expositors observe the let him not approach: not to draw near to the altar. The expositors observe the to offer the bread of God: the dignity of the service guarded. The believer is taught to mark the whosoever of thy seed, the that hath any blemish, the let him not approach, the to offer the bread of God, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that no blemished son of Aaron might offer the bread of God — the dignity of the altar guarded, pointing to the one perfect Priest without blemish (Heb. 9:14).
18:
"For whatsoever man he be that hath a blemish, he shall not approach: a blind man, or a lame, or he that hath a flat nose, or any thing superfluous," The witnesses note the first blemishes named — blindness, lameness, a flat nose, or any superfluous member — each barring from the altar. The witnesses note that Gill notes the bar held whether the blemish were fixed from birth or but transient, while it remained on him. The witnesses note that Clarke reads the blind as blind in one eye, the wholly blind being incapable of the service at all. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a blind man — barred, the wholly blind incapable of service; the or a lame — the lame not to approach the altar; the a flat nose — the disfigured face among the blemishes; the anything superfluous — any excess of member a blemish.

The witnesses agree the catalogue was no scorn of the afflicted but a sign: the visible wholeness of the priest taught that those who serve at God's altar should be entire, lacking nothing — a shadow of the spiritual soundness required of all who minister, and supremely fulfilled in Christ, in whom was no defect at all.

So the witnesses make the verse the first blemishes: The witnesses note the a blind man: barred, the wholly blind incapable of service. The witnesses note the or a lame: the lame not to approach the altar. The witnesses note the a flat nose: the disfigured face among the blemishes. The witnesses note the anything superfluous: any excess of member a blemish. The expositors observe the a blind man: barred, the wholly blind incapable of service. The expositors observe the or a lame: the lame not to approach the altar. The expositors observe the a flat nose: the disfigured face among the blemishes. The expositors observe the anything superfluous: any excess of member a blemish. The believer is taught to mark the a blind man, the or a lame, the a flat nose, the anything superfluous, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that blindness, lameness, and disfigurement barred from the altar — the priest's wholeness a sign of the soundness required of those who serve God, fulfilled in Christ without defect.
19:
"Or a man that is brokenfooted, or brokenhanded," The witnesses note the broken foot or hand among the blemishes — the priest with a maimed or ill-set limb barred from the service. The witnesses note that Gill reads it of any bone or joint of hand or foot broken or distorted, the clubfooted or crook-fingered, unfit to ascend the altar and lay the sacrifice in order. The witnesses note that Clarke and Ellicott read the badly cured fracture or dislocation of foot or hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brokenfooted — a maimed or ill-set foot; the or brokenhanded — a broken or dislocated hand; the unfit for the altar — unable to lay the sacrifice in order; the the emblem — of those disorderly in walk and unfit for good work.

The witnesses agree the maimed hand or foot pictured a deeper unfitness: Gill draws the emblem of those disorderly in their walk and unfit for every good work; the priest's sound limbs taught that those who handle holy things and walk before God should be whole in conduct as in body.

So the witnesses make the verse the maimed limb: The witnesses note the brokenfooted: a maimed or ill-set foot. The witnesses note the or brokenhanded: a broken or dislocated hand. The witnesses note the unfit for the altar: unable to lay the sacrifice in order. The witnesses note the the emblem: of those disorderly in walk and unfit for good work. The expositors observe the brokenfooted: a maimed or ill-set foot. The expositors observe the or brokenhanded: a broken or dislocated hand. The expositors observe the unfit for the altar: unable to lay the sacrifice in order. The expositors observe the the emblem: of those disorderly in walk and unfit for good work. The believer is taught to mark the brokenfooted, the or brokenhanded, the unfit for the altar, the the emblem, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the broken hand or foot barred from the altar — an emblem of those disorderly in walk, the priest to be whole in conduct as in body.
20:
"Or crookbackt, or a dwarf, or that hath a blemish in his eye, or be scurvy, or scabbed, or hath his stones broken;" The witnesses note the further blemishes — the hunchbacked, the dwarf, the diseased eye, the scurvy, scabbed, or maimed — completing the catalogue. The witnesses note that Gill reads "crookbackt" of one with a hunch, and notes the Jewish glosses on the eye and brow defects. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch gather the list of bodily imperfections that unfitted a priest for the altar. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the crookbackt or dwarf — the deformed of frame; the a blemish in the eye — the diseased or defective eye; the scurvy or scabbed — the diseased of skin; the the maimed — any bodily imperfection barring from the altar.

The witnesses agree the full and particular list pressed the one lesson home: nothing maimed or diseased might come near to offer, for the altar of the holy God demanded the unblemished; yet the very strictness, kept up through so long a catalogue, magnifies the grace by which the spotless Christ became our Priest, the only one able to answer the law's whole demand.

So the witnesses make the verse the full catalogue: The witnesses note the crookbackt or dwarf: the deformed of frame. The witnesses note the a blemish in the eye: the diseased or defective eye. The witnesses note the scurvy or scabbed: the diseased of skin. The witnesses note the the maimed: any bodily imperfection barring from the altar. The expositors observe the crookbackt or dwarf: the deformed of frame. The expositors observe the a blemish in the eye: the diseased or defective eye. The expositors observe the scurvy or scabbed: the diseased of skin. The expositors observe the the maimed: any bodily imperfection barring from the altar. The believer is taught to mark the crookbackt or dwarf, the a blemish in the eye, the scurvy or scabbed, the the maimed, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the full list of blemishes pressed home one lesson — nothing maimed or diseased to come near the altar, magnifying the grace by which the spotless Christ became our Priest.
21:
"No man that hath a blemish of the seed of Aaron the priest shall come nigh to offer the offerings of the LORD made by fire: he hath a blemish; he shall not come nigh to offer the bread of his God." The witnesses note the rule gathered into one: no blemished son of Aaron, high or common, might come near to offer the offerings of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Jewish reckoning of the blemishes to the number of one hundred and forty, any one of which barred a priest. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the rule for all the seed of Aaron afflicted with bodily imperfection. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no blemished man — none of Aaron's seed with a defect; the come not nigh to offer — barred from the act of offering; the the offerings made by fire — the altar belonging to the unblemished; the he hath a blemish — the principle fixed by the repeated word.

The witnesses agree the repetition seals the law: the blemish, whatever it be, kept a man from the act of offering, however true his descent or office; the altar belonged to the unblemished, and the very emphasis ("he hath a blemish") fixes the principle that drawing near to offer demands a wholeness no fallen man can bring of himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the rule sealed: The witnesses note the no blemished man: none of Aaron's seed with a defect. The witnesses note the come not nigh to offer: barred from the act of offering. The witnesses note the the offerings made by fire: the altar belonging to the unblemished. The witnesses note the he hath a blemish: the principle fixed by the repeated word. The expositors observe the no blemished man: none of Aaron's seed with a defect. The expositors observe the come not nigh to offer: barred from the act of offering. The expositors observe the the offerings made by fire: the altar belonging to the unblemished. The expositors observe the he hath a blemish: the principle fixed by the repeated word. The believer is taught to mark the no blemished man, the come not nigh to offer, the the offerings made by fire, the he hath a blemish, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that no blemished priest might come near to offer — the altar belonging to the unblemished, drawing near demanding a wholeness no fallen man can bring of himself.
22:
"He shall eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy, and of the holy." The witnesses note the grace within the law: though barred from offering, the blemished priest still ate the bread of his God, both the most holy and the holy portions. The witnesses note that Gill notes their natural infirmities did not make them impure, morally or ceremonially, so they might eat of the sacrifices that impure persons might not. The witnesses note that, being priests with no inheritance nor livelihood, provision was made that they might not perish for defects not of their own making. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he shall eat — the blemished priest still fed at God's table; the the most holy and the holy — his full priestly portion not withheld; the not impure — his infirmity no moral or ceremonial defilement; the provision made — that he perish not for a defect not his own.

The witnesses agree this tempers the strictness with tender mercy: the blemished priest was not cast out of the family of God nor stripped of his portion, but fed at the holy table though kept from the holy service — a beautiful figure that the weak and unfit are still owned and provided for by God, though not all are called to every office.

So the witnesses make the verse the grace in the law: The witnesses note the he shall eat: the blemished priest still fed at God's table. The witnesses note the the most holy and the holy: his full priestly portion not withheld. The witnesses note the not impure: his infirmity no moral or ceremonial defilement. The witnesses note the provision made: that he perish not for a defect not his own. The expositors observe the he shall eat: the blemished priest still fed at God's table. The expositors observe the the most holy and the holy: his full priestly portion not withheld. The expositors observe the not impure: his infirmity no moral or ceremonial defilement. The expositors observe the provision made: that he perish not for a defect not his own. The believer is taught to mark the he shall eat, the the most holy and the holy, the not impure, the provision made, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the blemished priest, though barred from the altar, still ate the bread of his God — the weak owned and provided for, though not called to every office.
23:
"Only he shall not go in unto the vail, nor come nigh unto the altar, because he hath a blemish; that he profane not my sanctuaries: for I the LORD do sanctify them." The witnesses note the limit: the blemished priest might eat, but not go in to the veil nor near the altar, lest he profane the sanctuary. The witnesses note that Gill explains he might not enter the holy place before the veil to set the shewbread, light the lamps, or offer incense, nor come to the altar. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the bar from the veil and the altar that he profane not the sanctuaries. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat, but not serve — fed at the table, kept from the holy work; the not in unto the veil — barred from the holy place and its service; the not nigh the altar — not to offer at the altar; the profane not my sanctuaries — the LORD's holy things guarded.

The witnesses agree the line is drawn with both holiness and kindness: the blemished priest keeps his place in the household and at the table, yet may not handle the holiest things, for the LORD who sanctifies the sanctuary will not have it served by the defective; holiness and mercy meet — the man honoured, the altar guarded.

So the witnesses make the verse the drawn line: The witnesses note the eat, but not serve: fed at the table, kept from the holy work. The witnesses note the not in unto the veil: barred from the holy place and its service. The witnesses note the not nigh the altar: not to offer at the altar. The witnesses note the profane not my sanctuaries: the LORD's holy things guarded. The expositors observe the eat, but not serve: fed at the table, kept from the holy work. The expositors observe the not in unto the veil: barred from the holy place and its service. The expositors observe the not nigh the altar: not to offer at the altar. The expositors observe the profane not my sanctuaries: the LORD's holy things guarded. The believer is taught to mark the eat, but not serve, the not in unto the veil, the not nigh the altar, the profane not my sanctuaries, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that the blemished priest might eat but not serve — holiness and mercy meeting, the man honoured at the table and the altar guarded from defect.
24:
"And Moses told it unto Aaron, and to his sons, and unto all the children of Israel." The witnesses note the close of the chapter: Moses delivered all these laws to Aaron, his sons, and all Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes he told them concerning defilement for the dead, marriages, and blemishes, that the priests be careful and the people know who was fit to offer for them. The witnesses note that the people too were charged, that they might bring their sacrifices only to fit hands. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses told it — the whole law delivered; the to Aaron and his sons — the priests charged to keep it; the and to all Israel — the people made to know who was fit to serve; the the common stake — the holiness of the altar the whole nation's concern.

The witnesses agree the publishing to all Israel gave the whole nation a stake in the holiness of the priesthood: not the priests only but the people were to guard the purity of the altar, knowing into whose hands they put their offerings — the holiness of the worship the concern of the whole congregation, under the LORD who sanctifies.

So the witnesses make the verse the law published: The witnesses note the Moses told it: the whole law delivered. The witnesses note the to Aaron and his sons: the priests charged to keep it. The witnesses note the and to all Israel: the people made to know who was fit to serve. The witnesses note the the common stake: the holiness of the altar the whole nation's concern. The expositors observe the Moses told it: the whole law delivered. The expositors observe the to Aaron and his sons: the priests charged to keep it. The expositors observe the and to all Israel: the people made to know who was fit to serve. The expositors observe the the common stake: the holiness of the altar the whole nation's concern. The believer is taught to mark the Moses told it, the to Aaron and his sons, the and to all Israel, the the common stake, and to look to the great High Priest who is holy. The verse thus records that Moses published the law to priests and people alike — the holiness of the priesthood the concern of the whole congregation, under the LORD who sanctifies.
